label: Unconfirmed or unclear issue
whatItOftenMeans: Without footage, this can still range from maintenance, blockage, or a localized defect to a broader structural problem.
whyItGetsExpensive: Costs usually jump only after footage shows a longer run, difficult access, or a problem that is broader than one easy repair.
upgradeSignal: Upgrade the concern when multiple fixtures are involved, backups repeat, or footage starts showing structural damage instead of a one-off clog.
downgradeSignal: Downgrade the concern when cleaning resolves the issue and footage does not show a recurring structural or material failure pattern.
trenchlessRead: Too early to judge trenchless until layout, access, and the actual damage pattern are documented.

label: Root intrusion
whatItOftenMeans: Roots often start as a maintenance or entry-point problem, but repeated intrusion can point to an opening or weak joint that keeps coming back.
whyItGetsExpensive: The bill grows when roots are dense, the opening is structural, or the rest of the run is old enough that cutting roots does not solve the real problem.
upgradeSignal: Upgrade concern when roots recur quickly, backups follow heavy use, or footage shows joint separation, cracking, or a longer damaged section.
downgradeSignal: Downgrade concern when roots look isolated, flow clears, and the surrounding pipe still looks serviceable.
trenchlessRead: Sometimes lining can make sense after cleaning and mapping, but only if the host pipe is still sound enough to rehabilitate.

label: Pipe belly or sag
whatItOftenMeans: A belly often means the pipe no longer maintains the grade needed to move waste and water cleanly, so recurring standing water or debris catching becomes the real issue.
whyItGetsExpensive: It gets expensive when the sag is long enough that cleaning does not hold, excavation becomes necessary, or the affected run sits under slab or hardscape.
upgradeSignal: Upgrade concern when the belly traps solids, backups recur, or the run is long enough that spot correction starts looking unrealistic.
downgradeSignal: Downgrade concern when the sag is minor, isolated, and not clearly affecting flow or solids movement.
trenchlessRead: Lining does not fix a meaningful grade problem by itself, so bellies often stay closer to excavation or partial replacement than trenchless rehab.

label: Offset joint or crack
whatItOftenMeans: Offsets and cracks can be minor watch-items or the first sign that the line has shifted enough to catch debris, invite roots, or weaken a section.
whyItGetsExpensive: Costs rise when the offset affects flow, roots keep using the opening, or the damaged area sits in a hard-to-reach part of the run.
upgradeSignal: Upgrade concern when flow is visibly disrupted, roots recur, or several nearby defects make the issue look less isolated.
downgradeSignal: Downgrade concern when the offset is small, not debris-catching, and the rest of the line still looks stable.
trenchlessRead: Localized trenchless rehab can work in some runs, but layout, access, and the true length of the damaged section still decide whether that is realistic.

label: Cast iron deterioration
whatItOftenMeans: Cast iron can range from rough but still working to materially scaled, offset, or deteriorated enough that the problem is broader than one isolated repair.
whyItGetsExpensive: Old cast iron gets expensive when deterioration is systemic, the run is under slab, or restoration and access dominate the job more than the pipe itself.
upgradeSignal: Upgrade concern when footage shows scale loss, repeated backups, multiple weak sections, or under-slab access that makes even a limited repair disruptive.
downgradeSignal: Downgrade concern when deterioration looks surface-level and the rest of the run still appears structurally intact.
trenchlessRead: Cast iron can still support lining or bursting in the right run, but method fit depends heavily on how much of the host pipe is truly serviceable.

label: Orangeburg or likely Orangeburg
whatItOftenMeans: Orangeburg usually raises replacement bias because the material itself can deform and fail in ways that make repeat repair logic hard to trust.
whyItGetsExpensive: Once the material is confirmed, the conversation often shifts from one defect to the reliability of the rest of the run and whether replacement is cleaner than repeated fixes.
upgradeSignal: Upgrade concern when deformation, flattening, collapse, or repeated root entry suggest the material is failing across more than one section.
downgradeSignal: Downgrade concern only if the material is not actually confirmed or the issue turns out to be a shorter isolated transition section.
trenchlessRead: Pipe bursting is sometimes a better trenchless candidate than lining when the host pipe is too deformed to trust as a rehabilitation shell.

label: Collapse or likely structural failure
whatItOftenMeans: Collapse moves the issue toward structural failure rather than routine maintenance, but footage still matters for how much of the run is affected.
whyItGetsExpensive: Costs spike because collapse often forces excavation, replacement, or longer-run work instead of cleaning or a small repair.
upgradeSignal: Upgrade concern quickly when footage confirms structural failure, backups are severe, or the damaged section sits in a hard-access part of the property.
downgradeSignal: Downgrade concern only when the collapse label turns out to be symptom-driven shorthand without confirming footage.
trenchlessRead: Trenchless can stay on the table only if the surrounding run and layout still support it. Many collapse scenarios still lean toward excavation.
